html:
XVII

When Merrick arrived at his apartment shortly after midnight, he slipped out of his dinner clothes and into a dressing-gown, and told Matsu to toss another chunk of pine on the fire and go to bed.

The eveningвАЩs entertainment had been a gusty symphony set to every key and tempo; brief passages of ineffable tenderness, momentary measures of hope, drably padded intervals of manoeuvring and modulation, spiced, with occasional breathtaking crescendos that went ripping shrilly up the chromatic scale precariously freighted with anxiety. The finale, unfortunately, had brought up on a most disquieting diminished chordвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Considering the evening, in total, its moods were as fitful and erratic as SibeliusвАЩ Valse Triste.

He had hoped to bring to it the sportsmanly spirit of an athlete entering upon a vigorous contest. His dear antagonist had promised that nobodyвБ†вАФnot even heвБ†вАФshould be aware of her irritation and resentment, and he knew she would keep her word. If hers would be a difficult part to play, his would be more so. All she had to do was to register cordiality. Whoever, with any social experience at all, had not learned how to dissemble in pretence of amiability when thrown with persons whom one disliked? As for him, the part he had drawn called for a cool casualness; not the stiff restraint of renunciation, or a show of ascetic indifference, but the calm courtesy of a man toward a woman he had barely met. It would be his task to carry this off convincinglyвБ†вАФthis with a heart aflame.

As he gave himself a brief inspection in the cheval glass before starting down town, he seriously pledged his own reflection that he would maintain an attitude of dignified chivalry toward the woman whose promised pretence of friendship, for an evening, would probably be little short of torture. And he had seen through, almost valorouslyвБ†вАФall but to the end.

And yet, remorseful as he was over that one brief but utter breakdown of self-discipline, which now made their relations more difficult than ever, he tingled to his nervesвАЩ ends with memories of those few enchanted moments when, even fully aware that she but kept her contract, her comradeship had seemed sincere.

In the mood of a miser, eager to be alone to finger his gold, he impatiently dismissed the solicitously lingering Matsu, lighted his pipe, and eased himself into a deep chair before the crackling fire, determined to live the evening over, item by item, and recover its most stirring sensations.

Pursuant to instructions from Joyce, he had called for Nancy Ashford. Regally stunning in crimson, with her youthful face, glistening white hair, superb figure and resilient step, Nancy was worth all the pride he had in her. He told her so, and she thanked himвБ†вАФfor that, and the flowers.

Nothing ever escaped her eye. She remarked, as he helped her into the limousine, that Richard had a new cap and puttees.

вАЬYou havenвАЩt had him in uniform before, have you?вАЭ she inquired. вАЬI thought you had some democratic convictions on the subject.вАЭ

вАЬSo I had,вАЭ he admitted, вАЬbut IвАЩve changed my mind. HeвАЩs part of an institution, with his uniform on, and it helps him keep out of mischief. At least, thatвАЩs the theory. Besides, he likes it.вАЭ

There had been a lot of rambling chatter like that, to which he had contributed with unusual animation; but Nancy dodged from under the fusillade of inconsequential talk presently, eager to be enlightened.

вАЬBobby,вАЭ she said, as his beautiful new car had swung into the current of boulevard traffic, and lengthened its stride, вАЬsomething tells me this is to be a rather difficult event.вАЭ

вАЬYou always were a keen observer, Nancy,вАЭ he conceded.

вАЬOne didnвАЩt have to be gifted with occult powers to see that the atmosphere at Brightwood, this afternoon, was heavily charged.вАЭ

вАЬPardon the interruption, butвБ†вАФI like you tremendously in that colour. You are very beautiful tonight, dear.вАЭ

вАЬMeaning that you donвАЩt want to tell me about it?вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФperhapsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Something like that.вАЭ

вАЬVery good, then. I shall shut my eyes, ears, and mouth. I am the three wise monkeys. IвАЩll pretend I donвАЩt know that you two have had a quarrel.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs a dear!вАЭ

вАЬAnd I shall also pretend I donвАЩt know that you silly things are so deeply in love youвАЩre afraid to exchange glances for fear your secret may be discovered.вАЭ

вАЬI still think you are beautiful, Nancy.вАЭ

вАЬBut dumb!вАЭ

вАЬNo, no! Not that!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Quite exasperatingly to the contrary!вАЭ

At this she had forgiven him, squeezed his hand, called him her dear boy; and, for the rest of the trip, discussed hospital affairs in a most businesslike manner.

They stepped out, at the Book-Cadillac, into a big-flaked snowstorm, and hurried to cover through the revolving doors. Joyce and Helen were awaiting them, by appointment, on the mezzanine, Joyce almost boisterously gay, swishy and sinuous in some green taffeta confection, wearing the corsage he had sent her; and, unless he was much mistaken, she had tucked a cocktail or two under his orchids, for there was a taut nervousness in her canary gestures and a strident overtone in her voice hardly to be had for less bother than the embarkation of about three jiggers of ginвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If Helen observed it, she was apparently resolved to ignore it. Seeing it was an occasion for the wholesale ignoring of unpleasant facts, there was probably no reason why she should cavil at this oneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What an adorable creature she wasвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ in the black velvetвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and the pearlsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and his orchids. So she had actually consented to wear them!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Score one against the beloved enemy!

Curiously enough, Joyce, from the first moment, had seemed bent upon hurling them at each other, almost violentlyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Maybe it was the ginвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Perhaps her instinct told her there was some unspoken bond between them which it was her duty to make articulateвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Of course, it was always difficult to guess what, how, or whether Joyce was thinkingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But, whatever her motive, if she had one, she was at no pains to disguise her intention to make this little party the occasion for a rapid development of their budding acquaintanceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ as if it were some fungus that must mature now or never.

Indeed, she had been utterly ruthless. Over the salad, she had chinked a momentary gap in the conversation by murmuring to herself, in an exaggeratedly stilted style, вАЬMisвАСses Hudson!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DocвАСtor Merrick!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Dear me!вАЭвБ†вАФwith a shrug and a sighвБ†вАФвАЬI had hoped they might be Bobby and Helen by this time!вАЭ To which Helen had replied, leaning toward her, in an apologetic, maternal undertone, вАЬDrink your milk, little one. ThereвАЩs a good child!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They had laughed, their merriment in the nature of applause.

The hotel was suffocatingly crowded. Some big trade convention was on, and the public lounges and foyers were swarming with fussy fat men, wearing long blue and gold badges on their lapels, beads of perspiration on their foreheads. Dozens of them were milling about, bound in as many directions, teetering themselves crabwise athwart the current, begging pardons, right and left, in the tone of вАЬGangway!вАЭ Their corrugated brows certified that unless they managed to squeeze through, the whole enterprise, after all this trouble and expense, would be futile.

Their weary wives sagged in every available chair, conscious of their redundant knees, pecking at fresh marcels with nervous fingers. A few of the more intrepid attempted a languorous indifference toward their unaccustomed cigarettes which, however, they regarded gingerly and at armвАЩs length, as foolhardy urchins hold sputtering firecrackersвБ†вАФnonchalantly, but with secret concern at the tail of the eye.

Joyce had impetuously taken NancyвАЩs arm and led the way.

вАЬKeep close, you two,вАЭ she shrilled, over her shoulder, вАЬand donвАЩt get lost. We must hurry, so we will not be late for the theatre!вАЭвБ†вАФand had swept Nancy along into the squirming pack.

Bobby had offered his arm to Helen and she had taken it; not perfunctorily, but as if she wished to do soвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She really neednвАЩt have done itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Neither Joyce nor Nancy could observe themвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She might have ignored his gestureвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was not necessary she should play a part at that moment.

They were jostled in the crowd. He had drawn her closer to him, and she had respondedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ NoвБ†вАФit was not merely that she had been pressed against him from withoutвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She had respondedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ There was a difference. He had drawn her closer to him, and she had responded!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He relighted his pipe, mechanically, and absently held the match until it nipped his fingers.

She had responded so generously that he could feel the warm, soft contours of her against his armвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She neednвАЩt have done thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was not in the book of her playвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yet, it was what she would have done if there had been no estrangement between them; or would she?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Probably notвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was hard to think straight about this affair.

Until now, no words had passed between them except their brief salutation at meeting, and JoyceвАЩs patter had sent even that into eclipse. He felt he should be saying something to her. He despised dull commonplaces, but the silence must not be permitted to grow any longer.

Of a sudden, he had become audacious.

вАЬI led you this way, once, through a very dark lane,вАЭ he heard himself saying.

вАЬOhвБ†вАФwas it?вАЭ she laughed. вАЬI thought we went hand in hand. I felt like a little girl being led to her first day in the kindergarten.вАЭ

вАЬSoвБ†вАФyou do remember!вАЭ

вАЬRather! I donвАЩt know what I should have done that night without you.вАЭ And she had looked up into his face and smiled. He wondered if she could feel the pounding of his heartвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They were entering the dining-roomвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Joyce waved a hand from a table halfway the length of the room.

вАЬTell me something, while weвАЩre thinking about that,вАЭ continued Helen, confidentially. вАЬWhy didnвАЩt you let me drive you home, that night, or at least put you down as we passed your gate?вАЭ

вАЬBecause I preferred you shouldnвАЩt know me. I thought that if you knew, you mightвБ†вАФвАЭ He had broken off, lamely, groping for a word.

вАЬThat was a very appropriate way to begin a friendship like ours,вАЭ she said crisply, вАЬseeing it was destined to be full of little deceits and riddles.вАЭ

вАЬI am sorry,вАЭ he said. He must have appeared appallingly so.

вАЬWellвБ†вАФdonвАЩt be, then!вАЭ she commanded hotly, with a savage little tug at his arm. вАЬYou look like Hamlet! Grin, I tell you! Your Nancy Ashford knows well enough we have been quarrelling! I saw it in her face this afternoon. IвАЩm not going to do this farce all by myself!вАЭ

He had looked down into her big blue eyes, amazed at this outburst so startlingly out of keeping with her serene expression, and laughed aloud. As he reviewed it, now, he laughed again. It had been absurdвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ beyond belief!

вАЬWhatвАЩs the joke?вАЭ demanded Joyce, as the waiters drew their chairs.

вАЬLong story,вАЭ said Helen briefly, вАЬand the good man laughed at it once. I canвАЩt have him hearing it over again.вАЭ

Nancy looked puzzled. He was secretly pleased over her bewilderment, and amused to see that the little episode had put a crimp in her omniscience.

вАЬYour curriculum is all prescribed,вАЭ Joyce was saying, as the waiters hurried away, вАЬall but the dessert. ThatвАЩs an elective. Otherwise, you take what the institution thinks will be good for you. No, darling,вАЭ she added, turning to Helen, вАЬit isnвАЩt veal. I remembered that you had already worked off your credits, majoring in veal.вАЭ

вАЬDo tell us some of your other experiences abroad!вАЭ Nancy had begged. вАЬIвАЩm hoping to go over, for a summer, presently, and IвАЩm awfully keen on travel tales.вАЭ

How graciously she had complied! And how charmingly she talked of her impressions. Most of the larger cities he knew almost as he knew his own, but she had seen things that he had missedвБ†вАФintimate glimpses up narrow streets and into quaint shops where, it seems, she had frequently made the acquaintance of a whole familyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How tenderly she talked about little children!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬIn Assisi, I once made some wonderful friends that way, in a little shop,вАЭ she was saying. вАЬIвАЩm afraid I began visiting the Bordinis, at first, to improve my colloquial Italian. Of course, I always bought some trifle to pay for my tuition, or brought along something for the children; but after a while, I found myself going there because I liked them and really needed their friendship. And, one day, little Maria, about three, took dreadfully ill. For three weeks, she just hung on to the mere edge of life. They were all so terribly worried. And, not having anything very important to do, I was in and out, frequently, through those daysвБ†вАФвАЭ

She interrupted her story to unfasten from within the neck of her gown a little silver cross.

вАЬMariaвАЩs mother insisted on giving me this when I left Assisi.вАЭ

The trinket was passed around the table. When it came to him, he had inspected it, with a feeling of reverence. It was holyвБ†вАФfor many excellent reasons.

вАЬI did not want to take it,вАЭ pursued Helen, вАЬfor I am sure it was the most treasured thing she owned. It had been blessed by the Holy Father himself, when, as a young girl, in nineteen hundred, she had accompanied a pilgrimage to Rome.вАЭ

вАЬSo thatвАЩs why youвАЩre wearing that cheap little cross!вАЭ exclaimed Joyce. вАЬDoes it bring you good luck?вАЭ

Helen smiled.

вАЬPerhaps,вАЭ she answered. вАЬAt least, I like it better than any other jewellery I have.вАЭ

вАЬVery naturally,вАЭ commented Nancy understandingly.

Joyce was quite attentive.

вАЬYou must have been the familyвАЩs main prop, during their trouble, to earn their enchanted cross. LetвАЩs have the rest of the story. What all did you do for them while Maria was sick? Help keep store? Were you the nurse? Go on, darling! Tell us all about it!вАЭ

At that point, he had been unable to restrain himself. Quite to his own amazement, he had held up a protecting hand.

вАЬNo, no, Joyce! We really darenвАЩt ask Mrs.¬†Hudson to tell us that!вАЭ Instantly he had felt embarrassed by his own remark.

вАЬHow funny! Why shouldnвАЩt she tell us?вАЭ

He had turned to Helen, at that, and asked soberly, вАЬDid you ever tell anybody that story?вАЭ

вАЬNo! Now that you askвБ†вАФI donвАЩt believe I ever did.вАЭ

вАЬThen I wouldnвАЩt, if I were you. This is a very valuable little keepsake, and its chief charm is in the fact that nobody knows but you what you did to earn it.вАЭ

вАЬHow perfectly ridiculous, Bobby!вАЭ shouted Joyce. вАЬDid you know he was so superstitious, Nancy?вАЭ

вАЬI had suspected itвБ†вАФa time or two.вАЭ

Helen was regarding him with perplexed, wide eyes as he put the little fetish into her palm with a gesture that had been perilously close to a caress, for his fingertips had lingered there.

вАЬSometimes I have thought of sending it back to her. You seem to have ideas on this subject, Doctor Merrick. Perhaps you will tell me; should I?вАЭ

вАЬBy no means. It wouldnвАЩt be valuable to her any more if she accepted it. She really canвАЩt take it back now, you see, becauseвБ†вАФbecauseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

HelenвАЩs lips were parted, and she was a bit breathless as she urged, insistently, вАЬYes?вБ†вАФbecauseвБ†вАФbecause what?вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФbecauseвБ†вАФby this time she has possiblyвБ†вАФprobablyвБ†вАФused it all up, herself.вАЭ

She stared at him steadily for a moment, as if she had seen a ghost. Then, half-articulately, and for his ears only, she murmured, вАЬSoвБ†вАФthatвАЩsвБ†вАФwhatвБ†вАФthatвБ†вАФmeans!вАЭ

вАЬYesвБ†вАФexactly! ThatвАЩs what it means!вАЭ

Her eyes were misty and her fingers trembled as she refastened the little cross inside the neck of her gown.

вАЬIвАЩm glad you told me,вАЭ she said, under her breath. вАЬI have so often wondered.вАЭ

Joyce put down both hands with a sudden gesture of impatience.

вАЬWhat on earth are you people talking about!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you know, Nancy?вАЭ

вАЬOhвБ†вАФvaguely, I think,вАЭ she repliedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYou liked the little towns best, didnвАЩt you, Helen? LetвАЩs hear some more about them. There was Bellagio. Tell us about that.вАЭ

вАЬOh, do!вАЭ echoed Joyce. вАЬYou wrote such wonderful letters from there. What was the name of that little hotelвБ†вАФon the top of the hill?вАЭ

вАЬThe Villa Serbelloni?вАЭ Helen grew moody. вАЬYesвБ†вАФI quite liked it, at first; but I grew very lonely there. I became so unhappy I left, one afternoon, on a momentвАЩs impulseвБ†вАФin a drenching rain.вАЭ

вАЬWhyвБ†вАФwhat was the matter, darling?вАЭ inquired Joyce solicitously.

вАЬJust sheer loneliness! The season was over, really, and almost everybody had gone away. There was a young woman I had found congenial, but she turned out to be a writing person, and seeing that was what she was there for I couldnвАЩt impose myself on her when she needed all her time for writing; so, one dreadfully lonely, stormy day, I left.вАЭ

вАЬEver see any of her work?вАЭ Nancy wanted to know.

Helen shook her head.

вАЬPerhaps you should make some inquiries,вАЭ suggested Joyce. вАЬMaybe you figured in some of her tales, yourself. WouldnвАЩt it be odd to pick up a story and find oneself cavorting about in it?вАЭ

During all this Bellagio talk, Helen had addressed herself chiefly to the others. As she replied to JoyceвАЩs comment, however, she turned her eyes slowly in his direction.

вАЬItвАЩs quite possible I may have qualified for some minor part in a story; for I had been as garrulous as a high school girl before I discovered her occupation.вАЭ

вАЬI am sure you were the heroine of the piece,вАЭ he had declared stoutly. вАЬI would swear to that!вАЭ

вАЬYou seem as certain as if you really knew.вАЭ She had leaned slightly toward him. Her nearness gave him a chance to mutter, in an undertone, вАЬI do!вАЭ

The talk had drifted, then, to ships. Nancy was anxious to know all about voyages; what to wear, how much and whom to tip, how long in advance one should book passage to insure good space.

вАЬHelen had hers on a dayвАЩs notice, coming back,вАЭ remembered Joyce.

вАЬBut itвАЩs not always that way,вАЭ Nancy argued. вАЬI recall a quite hectic experience we had in getting accommodations for some friend of BobbyвАЩs who was suddenly required to go to Buenos Aires.вАЭ

He had glanced apprehensively at Helen, and found her staring into his eyes, her brows knitted in perplexity. Quickly collecting herself, she said:

вАЬPerhaps the season had something to do with the congestion. When was it?вАЭ

вАЬWhen was it, Bobby?вАЭ queried Nancy. вАЬYou ought to remember. You were no end excited over getting him off by that boat. It must have been about a year ago; possibly a little earlier than this.вАЭ

вАЬSomething like that,вАЭ he had agreed disinterestedly.

The waiters had handed them menu cards. Joyce and Nancy had their heads together in consultation over parfait flavours. Helen had raised her card until it screened her face from them.

вАЬThat was very good of you,вАЭ she said softly. вАЬI never guessedвБ†вАФuntil now.вАЭ

вАЬI didnвАЩt intend you should. I hope you will never give it another thought. IвАЩm sorry the matter inadvertently came up.вАЭ

Her face was studious for a moment; then brightened, suddenly, with illumination.

вАЬOhвБ†вАФI see!вАЭ she murmured.

вАЬI wonder if you do.вАЭ

She nodded her head vigorously.

вАЬIt is something likeвБ†вАФlike my BordinisвБ†вАФand my little cross, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ

вАЬYesвБ†вАФexactly like that!вАЭ

Joyce had put an end to their cryptic byplay with a demand for light on the dessert problemвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It had been a very tender moment. As he mulled it over now, analytically, it occurred to him that had he been called away, on some emergency duty, at that juncture, he might at this moment be exulting in the hope that their misunderstanding had been definitely clearedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He rose and paced the room, digging his fingertips into his temples, paused at the little table, refilled his pipe, replenished the grate, and sank again into his chair. A small cathedral clock on the mantel wearily tolled the first quarter.

Those four strokes, when, on occasion, they caught his attention, invariably sent a momentary cloud drifting across his mood. It was not so at the half. The clock seemed to have cheered up, noticeably, by that time. It was almost reassuring when it came to the third quarter. But, always, that jaded, resigned, mocking, Amarish da-de-di-dum at the first quarter impressed him with the solemn asininity of whatever he happened to be doing and the futility of everything he was planning to do. It was exactly as if Eternal Destiny stretched its long arms and yawned. He could never be sure precisely what it said. Sometimes the strokes were but four gradations of an articulate sigh of inexpressible fatigue.

The vibrations still lingered. He glanced up. It was fifteen minutes past twoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He resumed his reflections, moodily, realizing that his memories, from this point, would be disturbing.

The short trip to the nearby theatre had been uneventful, made in his own car. As it drew up at the kerb, he had heard Helen exclaim to Nancy, вАЬWhat a beautiful car! What is it?вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He had not caught NancyвАЩs reply; but she knew.

They were very late, having lingered long at dinnerвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Gropingly following the usherвАЩs little electric torch, they had dodged guiltily into their seats which fortunately were on the aisle.

A nimble chorus marched mincingly across the stage, single file, in close formation, like a garish caterpillar; coquetted, shrieked a piercing blast, broke ranks, and were joined by the male contingent which sauntered in from the wings. There was a stormy repetition of the theme song, a final deafening screech, with arms aloft, and the lights burst on as the curtain fell.

Joyce, who had insisted on leading the way, leaned to the left, across Nancy and Helen, to hand him the seat checksвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How vividly every trivial incident stood out now, chiselled in high reliefвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He had reached for the ticket-stubs, his movement pressing him close against HelenвАЩs bare shoulder. His hand had lightly brushed her arm. Every chance contact swept him with a suffocating surge of emotion. It was only by the sternest resolution that he resisted the urge to touch her.

He could not remember what the chatter was about at this first entrвАЩacte. Joyce seemed to have provided most of itвБ†вАФsome amusing incident of JasmineвАЩs opening night in New York. Nancy was her best listener; Helen smilingly half-attentive, half-preoccupied.

The orchestra trailed in, twitched its E-string; the director raised both hands, swept his crew fore and aft with a final inspection; and they were off at full gallop in the descending darkness.

He had wished he was not quite so acutely conscious of her beside him, fearing she might sense the physical out-groping of himself toward her. NancyвАЩs experienced observation recurred to him. He had told her how keenly aware he had been of the girl in the car beside him, that night in the country. Nancy had pooh-poohed his naive notion that Helen was, of course, ignorant of his sensations.

вАЬNonsense!вАЭ Nancy had scoffed. вАЬDo you imagine she could have that effect on you without sharing it?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ How little you know about women!вАЭ

It was near the close of the second act that the catastrophe occurred.

He had not been following the silly, threadbare plot with enough attention to realize what, if anything, it was aiming at. His mind had been concentrated on the magnetic presence beside him, what time he was not daydreaming of the happiness he would find in surrounding her with the things she ought to have. It was not until he had irretrievably blundered that he came awake to the fact that he had unwittingly insulted her.

The dashing ing√©nue had returnedвБ†вАФit was a colourful scene of a country house partyвБ†вАФin a luxurious limousine. The fact that she was penniless; that the car was the property of the brazen broker who had been pursuing her throughout the play with gifts and attentions obviously to be credited on account; that the imported gown she wore was his by purchaseвБ†вАФall this was of no significance to himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ At that moment, the only fact of any interest to him was the quite good-looking limousine.

Impetuously, he had turned to HelenвБ†вАФtheir heads had touched, lightly, for an instantвБ†вАФand whispered, вАЬI heard you say you liked my new car. IвАЩm not using it much. IвАЩd like to lend it to you for the time youвАЩre here.вАЭ

Perhaps, even then, the most serious blunder of all might have been avoided had she been quick with an emphatic refusalвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Unsuspecting that her silence meant nothing more encouraging than that she had been stunned by his raw audacity, and heartened by that misinterpreted silence, he had groped shyly, his heart pounding, for the hand that he knew lay, palm up, very white against the black velvet.

Perhaps she had intuitively divined his intentionвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Perhaps the slight movement of his arm gave her warningвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Or, did she choose that exact second to toy with her strand of pearls?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He would probably never know how it had happenedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The warm velvet stirred uneasily under his brief caress.

The curtain was falling. The house was flooded with light. He glanced apprehensively toward her. Her cheeks were flushed, and her little fist, tightly clutching her handkerchief, was pressed hard against her lips.

On JoyceвАЩs suggestion, they strolled in the lobby. On the way up the aisle, Helen had taken NancyвАЩs arm, and Joyce, observant, had tarried until he fell into step with her. Animatedly, she had carried the full responsibility for their desultory talk. He was glad, for his mind was in chaos.

When the signal summoned to the last act, they returned in the order in which they had made their exit, and when their seats were reached, Helen led the way in, leaving Joyce beside him.

What a beastly cad she must think him!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ButвБ†вАФsurely her own good sense would tell her he had not meant it!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Not that way!

He hadnвАЩt the faintest idea what the last act was about; sat through it suffering every imaginable torture. After a few eternities, the wretched thing was done.

Their parting was brief, conventional, without one single understanding look into each otherвАЩs eyes.

In the morning, he would find her and attempt an explanationвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The clock tolled the four quarters and struck threeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He had an operation to do at nine.

Wearily he flung off his clothes and went to bed. As he relaxed on his pillow, sick at heart, the chimes offered a cynical comment on his adroit handling of the eveningвАЩs complicated problem.

Upon his arrival at the hospital, in the morning, the desk notified him he was requested to call Mr.¬†Randall at the Fourth National Bank, to which he paid no attention.

Having finished his operation, he called the Statler and asked for Mrs.¬†Hudson. She had checked out.
